title: 9th Circuit Hears Arguments on Portland ICE Use-of-Force Injunctions; Judges Skeptical of DHS
excerpt: The U.S. Court of Appeals for the 9th Circuit heard oral arguments on whether to reinstate injunctions limiting federal agents' use of tear gas and less-lethal weapons at the Portland ICE facility, with judges appearing skeptical of the government's position that officers had broad discretion to use chemical munitions on nonviolent protesters.
content:
On April 7, 2026, a three-judge panel of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the 9th Circuit heard expedited oral arguments on the Trump administration's appeal of two federal district court orders limiting Homeland Security agents' use of tear gas, pepper balls, and other crowd-control weapons at Portland's ICE facility.

The lower-court injunctions, issued by Judges Michael Simon and Amy Baggio in March 2026, found that DHS had an unwritten policy of using excessive force on nonviolent protesters and that the repeated deployment of chemical munitions violated tenants' bodily integrity. Both judges had cited video evidence of agents spraying protesters at close range without warning. The Ninth Circuit had temporarily paused both injunctions on March 25, 2026, on a 2-1 vote.

  • During arguments, the panel appeared largely skeptical of DOJ's position, with even Trump-appointed Judge Eric Tung noting that past targeting of protesters had created a constitutional 'chill' on First Amendment rights.
  • ACLU attorneys pointed out the lower court found 'defendants have an unwritten policy of using excessive force against nonviolent peaceful protestors... for the purpose of chilling the constitutional rights to free speech.'
  • A decision was expected before mid-April 2026, when both lawsuits were to advance to the discovery stage.
  • Former CBP Commissioner Gil Kerlikowske told CNN: 'They have violated every protocol on less-lethal (weapons) and crowd control. Not making an announcement to disperse, body-slamming people to the ground...they're supposed to de-escalate, and they don't.'
mediaPlaceholder: Video submitted as court evidence showing federal agents deploying tear gas against nonviolent protesters outside Portland ICE facility
